data.title: Boeing ties employee incentive plan to company-wide performance
data.description:
Annual bonuses for more than 100,000 Boeing employees this year will be tied to company-wide performance, rather than by business unit, as in previous years.

Eighty percent of the bonuses are tied to Boeing's financial performance, with the remaining 20% tied to progress on improving safety and quality, and program execution, according to the 2025 proxy statement filed on Friday with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission.

The announced changes come two days after CEO Kelly Ortberg told employees the company's myriad business units have to work together as part of a wider cultural shift driven in large part by employee feedback that he said he expects to be "brutal to leadership."

Ortberg's total compensation in 2024 was about $18.4 million, including a $1.2 million signing bonus and $525,000 in salary since he joined the company in August. He also received $313,000 to relocate to Seattle to be closer to Boeing's commercial airplane production. The rest of his compensation was in equity grants.

Former CEO Dave Calhoun received $15 million in total compensation. He left the company in the wake of a mid-air panel blowout on a nearly new 737 MAX, which revealed widespread quality problems.

data.title: Robinhood to Pay $30 Million to Settle Finra Investigation — WSJ
data.description:
By Hannah Erin Lang

Two brokerage units of Robinhood Markets have agreed to pay $30 million to settle an investigation by the Financial Industry Regulatory Authority into a range of violations, some of which date back more than a decade.

Finra, Wall Street's self-regulator, found violations related to anti-money laundering programs, disclosures and clearing system supervision at Robinhood Securities and Robinhood Financial, the regulator said in a release Friday. Robinhood neither admitted nor denied wrongdoing.

"We are pleased to resolve these historical matters, many of which date as far back as 2014, and which Robinhood Securities and Robinhood Financial have since remediated," Erica Crosland, Robinhood's associate general counsel said in a statement.

Robinhood agreed to pay $3.8 million in restitution to customers affected by a policy known as order collaring, which occasionally prevented some users' trades from being executed. Robinhood ceased that practice in 2023.

Finra also said that Robinhood failed to reasonably supervise its clearing technology system, which regulators say contributed to the problems the brokerage faced in January 2021, at the height of the meme stock trading frenzy.

Friday's settlement is the latest in a string of penalties paid by Robinhood as it has evolved from a disruptive startup into a more established financial firm.

Last month, Robinhood said the Securities and Exchange Commission dropped an investigation into its crypto unit.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: News Highlights: Top Global Markets News of the Day - Friday at 5 PM ET
data.description:
Dow Trims Losses to End a Tough Week

Stocks rise after Powell comments, jobs report-but finish week down more than 2%.

Tariff Fights Keep the Fed in a Holding Pattern on Rates

Fed Chair Jerome Powell says central bank positioned "to wait for greater clarity" on economic outlook.

U.S. Added 151,000 Jobs Last Month

The U.S. continued to generate jobs at a steady pace in February, offering reassurances that the labor market has remained relatively stable since President Trump took office.

House Democrats Reject GOP Plan, Raising Government Shutdown Risk

The move comes after GOP leaders rejected their efforts to write in protections against further cuts to federal workers and services.

Canada to Offer C$6.5 Billion to Help Firms Mitigate Tariff Fallout

Canada is also making changes to its jobless-benefit plan, helping employers keep workers while reducing their hours because of trade disruption.

Justice Department Opens Probe of Sharp Surge in Egg Prices

Antitrust enforcers are investigating whether large producers have engaged in anticompetitive conduct.

Canada Unemployment Rate Held Steady at 6.6% in February

Employers in the country added just 1,100 jobs last month.

Eurozone Grew More Strongly Than Thought at End of 2024

The stronger-than-expected reading was thanks to signs of more robust growth in some members of the currency area, including Ireland and Italy.

German Factory Orders Slump Again as Hopes Grow for Stimulus to Counter Tariff Threat

The drop points to a continued slump in a sector whose hope for a rebound lies in lower interest rates and a bump in government spending.

You Can't Escape Politics. Your Investing Decisions Can.

Some ETFs match your portfolio to your ideology. There's just one problem: The stock market doesn't care how you vote.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Company News of the Day - Friday at 5 PM ET
data.description:
Activist Mantle Ridge Builds Over $1 Billion Stake in Tech-Services Firm Cognizant

The investor has been working behind the scenes at the tech-services firm and isn't currently planning to nominate directors.

Hudson's Bay, Canada's Oldest Retail Chain, Nears Bankruptcy

Department store operator is planning to file for insolvency after separating from Saks Global.

Canada's Big-Tobacco Settlement Finalized, Ending Decadeslong Litigation

Units of Philip Morris, British American Tobacco and Japan Tobacco will pay $22.7 billion to settle all smoking-related lawsuits in Canada.

Apple Says Some AI-Powered Enhancements to Siri to Be Delayed

The company, which has faced slumping iPhone sales in recent months, said that it would take it longer than expected to make Siri more personalized.

Wayfair to Cut 340 Tech Jobs as Part of AI, Cloud Shift

Wayfair said it anticipates to incrementally realize savings starting in the second half of 2025 and building into the beginning of 2026.

Walgreens Goes From $100 Billion Health Giant to Private-Equity Salvage Project

Sycamore's deal to take Walgreens private follows the storied chain's decline amid e-commerce and health-industry changes.

U.S. Likely to Ban Chinese App DeepSeek From Government Devices

The White House is weighing measures to restrict the artificial-intelligence upstart, including banning its chatbot from government devices.

Blackstone Raises Largest Commercial Property Debt Fund With $8 Billion Haul

The fund took two years to build and will be active in North America, Europe and Australia.

MDA Space Plans to Expand, Seeing Few Threats From Mounting Tariffs

MDA Space isn't worried about the threat of tariffs thanks to customers that extend far beyond its borders and its limited dependence on U.S. suppliers.

Global Insurer Allianz in Talks for $3 Billion-Plus Viridium Deal

Germany's Allianz is in exclusive talks to acquire Viridium, a consolidator of closed books of insurance policies, in a deal that would bolster its domestic operations.

data.title: Eli Lilly CEO's Total Compensation Rose 10% in 2024
data.description:
By Connor Hart

Eli Lilly Chief Executive David Ricks annual compensation rose to $29.2 million in 2024, a 10% increase from the $26.2 million he received a year earlier.

Ricks' payout included $19.7 million in stock awards, $5.7 million in bonuses and nearly $352,000 in other forms of compensation, according to the drugmaker's 2025 proxy statement filed Friday with the Securities and Exchange Commission. His base salary for the year was $1.7 million.

Bonuses were tied to performance. Eli Lilly said that executives' performance highlights last year included delivering a strong financial performance, with product revenue and earnings per share topping internal targets, as well as furthering its pipeline of treatments.

Lucas Montarce, who was appointed as Eli Lilly's top finance chief in September, received $2.9 million in total compensation in 2024.

Montarce succeeded Anat Ashkenazi, who left the company earlier in the year to become CFO of Google-parent Alphabet. For her work with Eli Lilly in 2024, Ashkenazi received $5 million in total compensation.

Gordon Brooks, who served as interim CFO for a period in 2024, as well as group vice president of corporate strategy and controller, notched $2.7 million in total compensation for the year.

Other executive payouts in 2024 included Chief Scientific Officer Daniel Skovronsky, who received $12.6 million; General Counsel and Secretary Anat Hakim, who received $7.5 million; and President of Loxo@Lilly Jake Van Naarden, who received $5.8 million.

Write to Connor Hart at connor.hart@wsj.com

data.title: DXP Enterprises Reports Fourth Quarter and Fiscal 2024 Results
data.description:
DXP Enterprises, Inc., a leading products and service distributor, has released its financial results for the fourth quarter and fiscal year ended December 31, 2024. The company reported significant growth in several key financial metrics, reflecting its robust performance and strategic initiatives over the past year.

Financial Highlights

For fiscal 2024, DXP Enterprises reported sales of $1.8 billion, marking a 7.4% increase from fiscal 2023. The company's full-year GAAP diluted earnings per share (EPS) stood at $4.22, with adjusted EPS at $4.51. Net income for the year was $70.5 million, up from $68.8 million in the previous year. Adjusted EBITDA reached $191.3 million, representing 10.6% of sales.

In the fourth quarter of 2024, sales were $470.9 million, compared to $407.0 million in the same period of 2023. The diluted EPS for the quarter was $1.29, with adjusted diluted EPS at $1.38. Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was $50.3 million, up from $41.9 million in Q4 2023.

Business and Operational Highlights

DXP Enterprises' business segments showed varied performance. The Service Centers segment reported revenue of $1.2 billion for the fiscal year, a 1.9% increase year-over-year, with a 14.3% operating income margin. The Innovative Pumping Solutions segment saw a substantial 47.7% increase in revenue to $323.0 million, with a 16.6% operating income margin. However, the Supply Chain Services segment experienced a slight decline in revenue by 1.5% to $256.4 million, with an 8.5% operating margin.

Strategic Initiatives and Corporate Developments

During fiscal 2024, DXP Enterprises completed seven acquisitions, contributing to its growth strategy. The company also refinanced its Senior Secured Term Loan B, raising $649.5 million and reducing borrowing costs by 100 basis points. Additionally, DXP repurchased 0.6 million shares for $28.8 million and ended the year with $148.4 million in cash and restricted cash.

Management's Perspective

David R. Little, Chairman and CEO, highlighted the strong performance across all business segments and the positive momentum going into 2025. He emphasized the company's balanced end markets and the successful execution of acquisitions as key drivers of growth. Kent Yee, CFO, noted that fiscal 2024 was the most profitable year in the company's history, with strong sales growth, operating leverage, and free cash flow generation.

Future Outlook

Looking ahead, DXP Enterprises is optimistic about its growth prospects in 2025. The company plans to leverage its strong balance sheet and broad product offering to drive both organic and acquisition-driven growth. Management expects continued positive dynamics in traditional end markets like oil & gas, as well as emerging opportunities in sectors such as water & wastewater.

SEC Filing: DXP ENTERPRISES INC [ DXPE ] - 8-K - Mar. 07, 2025

data.title: Battalion Oil Files 8K - Director, Officer or Compensation Filing >BATL
data.description:
Battalion Oil Corp. (BATL) filed a Form 8K - Director, Officer or Compensation Filing - with the U.S Securities and Exchange Commission on March 07, 2025.

Retention and Incentive Plan

On March 4, 2025, Battalion Oil Corporation (the "Company") adopted a Retention and Incentive Plan (the "Plan") to provide for certain compensation for retention and incentives to eligible employees of the Company, including the Company's named executive officers (each, a "Participant").

The Plan provides for the payment of cash bonuses (the "Retention Bonuses") to Participants who were employees at the time the Plan was implemented and who remain employed by the Company through December 31, 2026 (the "Retention Bonus Term"). The Retention Bonuses consist of two payments, subject to certain restrictions, the first payment is scheduled to occur in early March 2025, and the second will become payable at the end of the Retention Bonus Term. The Retention Bonuses are subject to the Participants' continued employment with the Company and would be owed back to the Company if the Participant leaves the Company without good reason or if their employment is terminated for cause before the Retention Bonus Term. Additionally, Participants will receive a partial advance payment on their 2025 bonus equal to 50% of their target bonus (the "2025 Bonus Prepayment"), which will be payable after the second quarter of 2025. The 2025 Bonus Prepayment is subject to the Participants' continued employment with the Company and would be owed back to the Company if the Participant leaves the Company without good reason or if their employment is terminated for cause before December 31, 2025.

Pursuant to the Plan, the Company established two bonus pools (the "Incentive Bonus Pools") to be distributed to certain key employees upon a change in control event, consisting of a "Base Pool" in the amount of $4 million and a "NIV Pool," that is based on growth of the value of the Company versus a base amount, and which funding would be determined by certain performance standards. The benchmark growth of the NIV Pool is calculated using a base amount and the sum of the net value received by the Company's equity holders in a change in control transaction and, using the XIRR function in Microsoft Excel, using those two values and February 28, 2025 and the closing date of the change in control transaction as the dates (the "IRR"). Upon such a qualifying change in control event, the NIV Pool would be funded as follows: (a) if the IRR exceeds a theoretical 10% (but less than 15%) then the NIV Pool shall be 10% of the positive difference between (i) sum of net value received equity holders and (ii) base amount (such difference being "Net Increase in Value"); (b) if the IRR exceeds a theoretical 15% (but less than 20%) then the incentive pool shall be 15% of the Net Increase in Value; and (c) if the IRR exceeds a theoretical 20% then the incentive pool shall be 20% of the Net Increase in Value. The Incentive Bonus Pools shall expire on December 31, 2026.

As a condition for the participation in the Plan, Participants shall agree to and waive all rights to the Equity Grant Units, awarded under the previously disclosed Merger Incentive Plan, dated as of September 19, 2024, which would have vested upon a change in control event. Upon mutual agreement by the Participant and the Company, such awards will be rescinded and deemed null and void, and the associated Equity Grant Units will be cancelled.

Following the adoption of, and pursuant to, the Plan, each of the Company's named executive officers, identified below, entered into individual retention and award agreements, and the Company allocated the following retention and incentive awards to such officers:

Named Executive Officer

Matthew B. Steele

Daniel P. Rohling

Walter R. Mayer

Retention Bonus – First Payment

$ 137,500

$ 145,000

$ 75,000

Retention Bonus – Second Payment

$ 137,500

$ 145,000

$ 75,000

2025 Bonus Prepayment

$ 183,750

$ 183,750

$ 106,622

Base Pool Percentage Share

31.75%

25%

11.25%

NIV Pool Percentage Share

30%

25%

6.5%

Under the Plan, the Board, upon recommendation of the Compensation Committee, directed management to explore paths to provide for a repurchase from Participants of the common stock issued upon vesting of restricted stock units, to occur in two phases, and subject to Participant's continued employment at such time: 50% of such shares in the near term and 50% on or around December 31, 2026. In each case, the purchase price would be $3.00 per share. As of the date of this report, management has not taken any substantive steps to implement the Board's directive.

The full text of this SEC filing can be retrieved at: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1282648/000155837025002511/batl-20250304x8k.htm

Any exhibits and associated documents for this SEC filing can be retrieved at: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1282648/000155837025002511/0001558370-25-002511-index.htm

Public companies must file a Form 8-K, or current report, with the SEC generally within four days of any event that could materially affect a company's financial position or the value of its shares.

data.title: Hudson's Bay, Canada's Oldest Retail Chain, Nears Bankruptcy — WSJ
data.description:
By Alexander Gladstone, Soma Biswas and Jodi Xu Klein

Hudson's Bay, the Canadian department store chain founded in 1670, is preparing for a bankruptcy filing within days, people familiar with the matter said.

The chain became a stand-alone business following its December 2024 deal to spin off its Saks Fifth Avenue subsidiary through a combination with Neiman Marcus Group. As part of that transaction, Saks acquired Neiman Marcus for $2.65 billion, establishing a new entity called Saks Global, which also owns Bergdorf Goodman.

Saks Global doesn't plan to file for bankruptcy, people familiar with the matter said. A spokesperson for Hudson's Bay declined to comment.

Hudson's Bay, based in Toronto, operates more than 80 stores and is one of the oldest companies in North America, tracing its roots to the fur trade during colonial times.

Hudson's Bay later transitioned into a retail giant. Its striped wool blanket, still sold today, is an emblem of Canada. The blankets were initially traded for beaver pelts in the 1700s.

Hudson's Bay has been owned since 2008 by NRDC Equity Partners, a private investment firm controlled by the American retail and real estate mogul Richard Baker, who has a record of scooping up retailers and often profiting from their valuable real-estate holdings.

In 2013, Hudson's Bay acquired Saks Fifth Avenue, combining it with Lord & Taylor to create one of the largest retailers in North America. Baker's firm later sold Lord & Taylor to Le Tote, after which both Lord & Taylor and Le Tote filed for bankruptcy. Baker has said he made profits far exceeding his original investment by selling some Lord & Taylor real-estate assets.

Retailers in Canada are facing pressure from inflation and high interest rates that have squeezed household budgets. Shoppers are cutting back on discretionary spending to focus on essentials and trading down to discount chains.

Hudson's Bay has been laying off corporate and store-level staff for more than a year, according to the Globe & Mail.

In 2023, the company raised roughly $350 million through the sale of real estate to fund store operations.

Write to Alexander Gladstone at alexander.gladstone@wsj.com, Soma Biswas at soma.biswas@wsj.com and Jodi Xu Klein at jodi.klein@wsj.com

data.title: US will use stablecoins to ensure dollar hegemony — Scott Bessent
data.description:
United States Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said the US government will use stablecoins to ensure that the US dollar remains the world’s global reserve currency during the White House Crypto Summit on March 7.

Bessent reiterated the Trump administration’s promise to end the war on crypto and committed to rolling back previous IRS guidance and punitive regulatory measures. Bessent then turned his attention to stablecoins and said:

"We are going to put a lot of thought into the stablecoin regime, and as President Trump has directed, we are going to keep the US [dollar] the dominant reserve currency in the world, and we will use stablecoins to do that."

President Trump told the summit that he hopes lawmakers will get a comprehensive stablecoin regulatory bill to his desk before the August Congressional recess.

The President was also critical of the Biden administration for selling portions of the seized Bitcoin , which he said amounted to billions in losses through premature selling.

Many of the attendees of the first White House Crypto Summit remarked on the historic nature of the event, which cements a seismic shift in the US government’s stance toward the digital asset industry.

Stablecoins as a way to extend US dollar hegemony

Overcollateralized stablecoins, which use short-term US Treasury bills and cash deposits to back their digital fiat tokens and thus drive demand for US debt instruments, have been pitched as a way to extend US dollar dominance.

Federal Reserve Governor Christopher Waller also voiced support for using stablecoins to prop up the dollar in February 2024.

Waller argued that the corrosive effect of cryptocurrencies on the market share of the US dollar would be mitigated by stablecoin demand.

In February 2025, Waller reiterated his stance that stablecoins could help preserve the dollar's status as the global reserve currency by overcoming capital controls in foreign countries and enhancing payment rails.

As part of this effort to leverage stablecoins to defend the US dollar, US representatives French Hill and Bryan Steil introduced a stablecoin bill titled the Stable Act of 2025 to establish a comprehensive regulatory framework for dollar-pegged digital fiat tokens.

data.title: Gene Hackman Died of Heart Disease, Wife of Rodent-Borne Illness, Officials Say — WSJ
data.description:
By Victoria Albert and Betsy McKay

Gene Hackman, the actor who was found dead in his home with his wife last week, died of heart disease, officials in New Mexico said.

Hackman's wife, Betsy Arakawa, died of hantavirus pulmonary syndrome, said Heather Jarrell, New Mexico's chief medical examiner, on Friday. Jarrell said the autopsy of Hackman, who was 95, also revealed the actor had advanced Alzheimer's disease.

Hackman's autopsy showed severe heart disease and evidence of prior heart attacks, Jarrell said. The actor tested negative for hantavirus.

Santa Fe County Sheriff Adan Mendoza said Arakawa was last seen returning home on the night of Feb. 11, and apparently died soon after. Officials said they believe Hackman survived for about another week, with the last registered activity on his pacemaker occurring Feb. 18.

According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, humans contract hantavirus from rodents such as rats and mice, especially when exposed to their urine, droppings and saliva. The virus causes fatigue, fever and muscle aches, as well as headaches, dizziness and stomach problems. Some people can develop shortness of breath, cough and fluid in the lungs. Hantavirus has a high mortality rate: About four in 10 of those who suffer from lung symptoms die, according to the CDC.

In New Mexico, the virus is spread by deer mice, which are common there. There have been 129 cases of hantavirus in New Mexico since 1993, including seven in 2023, according to the CDC.

An inspection of the couple's property found that the risk of hantavirus exposure in the main house was low, but that there were signs of rodent entry in other structures on the property, officials said at a press conference.

Write to Victoria Albert at victoria.albert@wsj.com and Betsy McKay at betsy.mckay@wsj.com

data.title: Profound Medical Corp. SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Profound Medical Corp., a leading medical device company specializing in MRI-guided ablation systems, has released its annual Form 10-K report for the fiscal year ending December 31, 2024. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges it faces in the competitive medical device industry.

Financial Highlights

Profound Medical Corp. reported significant financial growth in 2024, driven by increased revenue and improved manufacturing efficiency. Key financial metrics include:

  • Revenue: $10.68 million, reflecting a 48% increase from the previous year due to higher recurring revenue and capital sales in the United States.
  • Gross Profit: $7.037 million, representing a 63% increase from the previous year, attributed to improved manufacturing efficiency and growth in capital system sales.
  • Operating Loss: $33.062 million, an increase from the previous year, driven by higher research and development expenses and selling, general and administrative expenses.
  • Net Loss: $27.816 million, slightly reduced from the previous year, with improvements in net finance income and foreign exchange gains.
  • Basic and Diluted Loss Per Share: $1.12, a decrease from $1.34 in the previous year, indicating a reduction in loss per share due to increased revenue and financial management.

Business Highlights

Profound Medical Corp. continues to make strides in its business operations, focusing on the commercialization of its innovative medical systems. Key business highlights include:

  • Business Model: The company operates a recurring revenue model in the United States, which includes durable hardware usage, one-time-use devices, and comprehensive clinical training services. In international markets, the business model consists of sales of durable goods and one-time-use devices for each patient treated.
  • Product Portfolio: The company focuses on two main systems: the TULSA-PRO system for prostate tissue ablation and the Sonalleve system for non-invasive treatment of uterine fibroids and other conditions. Both systems are designed to be used with MRI scanners and are compatible with select MRI scanners from Philips, Siemens, and GE Healthcare.
  • Regulatory Approvals: The TULSA-PRO system received FDA clearance in August 2019 and is CE marked in the EU. The Sonalleve system is CE marked in the EU and approved in China and South Korea. The company continues to pursue additional regulatory approvals in international jurisdictions.
  • Clinical Studies: The TULSA-PRO system has been evaluated in several clinical studies, including the TACT Pivotal Clinical Trial, which demonstrated its effectiveness in prostate cancer ablation with favorable safety and quality of life outcomes. The Sonalleve system has undergone studies for uterine fibroids and adenomyosis, showing promising results.
  • Geographical Performance: For the year ended December 31, 2024, approximately 78% of revenues were generated in the United States, 8% in the EU, and 14% in Asia. This reflects a strategic focus on the U.S. market, with limited commercial efforts in Europe.
  • Strategic Partnerships: The company has strategic collaborations with Philips and Siemens for lead generation and distribution of durable equipment. A co-sales and co-marketing agreement with Siemens Healthineers was announced in December 2024 to offer a complete solution for MRI-guided prostate therapy.
  • Future Outlook: The company plans to expand its commercial footprint through additional regulatory approvals, strategic acquisitions, and increased investment in research and development. The introduction of a capital sales model in the United States in 2024 aims to complement the existing recurring revenue model.

Strategic Initiatives

Profound Medical Corp. is actively pursuing strategic initiatives to enhance its market position and drive long-term growth. Key strategic initiatives include:

  • Commercialization Focus: The company is focused on the commercialization of its TULSA-PRO and Sonalleve systems. It is deploying a hybrid recurring revenue business model in the United States for TULSA-PRO, which includes a one-time payment model and a traditional capital sales model.
  • Exploring New Markets: The company is exploring additional potential treatment markets for Sonalleve, such as non-invasive ablation of abdominal cancers and hyperthermia for cancer therapy.
  • Capital Management: In 2024, Profound Medical Corp. raised significant capital through public offerings and a private placement, issuing a total of 8,425,039 common shares at $7.50 per share, resulting in gross proceeds of $63,188. The company also amended its credit agreement with CIBC, transitioning to a revolving line of credit with a maturity date of March 3, 2027, and an option to increase the revolving commitment by $5,000. The company has been actively managing its debt, with repayments of $2,560 in long-term debt during 2024.
  • Future Outlook: Profound Medical Corp. plans to use the proceeds from its recent equity offerings to fund the continued commercialization of the TULSA-PRO system in the United States and the global development and commercialization of both the TULSA-PRO and Sonalleve systems. The company expects its expenses to increase due to these commercialization efforts and is exploring additional capital sources to support its strategic initiatives. The company is also focused on maintaining compliance with its financial covenants under the CIBC Credit Agreement.

Challenges and Risks

Profound Medical Corp. faces several challenges and risks that could impact its business operations and financial performance. Key risks include:

  • Limited Operating History and Losses: The company has a limited operating history and has incurred significant losses since inception. It expects to continue incurring losses as it commercializes its TULSA-PRO system in the U.S. and expands its sales, marketing, and manufacturing capabilities.
  • Capital Intensive Business: The business requires substantial capital investment to increase commercial capacity and conduct R&D. The company may face challenges in securing financing on favorable terms, which could impact its ability to execute its business strategy.
  • Foreign Currency Risk: As the company commercializes its products in the U.S., it will face increased exposure to foreign currency risk, which could negatively impact financial results due to exchange rate fluctuations.
  • Reliance on Collaborative Partners: The company relies on partners like Philips, Siemens, and GE Healthcare for sales and marketing. Any disruption in these relationships could adversely affect commercialization efforts.
  • Market Acceptance: The success of the TULSA-PRO system and other products depends on achieving market acceptance, which could be impacted by factors such as safety, efficacy, cost-effectiveness, and reimbursement coverage.
  • Manufacturing and Supply Chain Risks: The company may face challenges in scaling manufacturing operations and relies on third-party manufacturers and single-source suppliers for components, which could affect its ability to meet demand.
  • Regulatory Risks: The company’s products are subject to extensive regulation. Delays or failures in obtaining necessary regulatory approvals could harm business prospects.
  • Competition: The company faces significant competition from established medical device companies with greater resources, which could impact its market share and profitability.
  • Data Integrity and Cybersecurity: Increasing dependence on information technology systems exposes the company to risks of data breaches and cyber-attacks, which could adversely affect operations.
  • Insurance Costs: Rising insurance costs, particularly for product liability, could negatively impact profitability.

Management acknowledges the challenges of achieving profitability and the need for significant investment in commercialization efforts. Strategies include building in-house sales and marketing capabilities and expanding manufacturing capacity. The company is also focused on securing reimbursement coverage to support market acceptance.

The company is exposed to market risks, including foreign currency exchange rate fluctuations, which may adversely affect financial results. It has not yet implemented hedging strategies to mitigate these risks.

SEC Filing: Profound Medical Corp. [ PRN ] - 10-K - Mar. 07, 2025

data.title: ORASURE TECHNOLOGIES INC SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
OraSure Technologies Inc., a leading provider of diagnostic products and sample collection devices, has released its 2024 Form 10-K report. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges it faces in the current market environment.

Financial Highlights

  • Net Revenues: $185.8 million, a decrease of 54% from $405.5 million in 2023, primarily due to a significant decline in COVID-19 diagnostics revenues.
  • Consolidated Gross Profit Margin: 43%, an increase from 42% in 2023, attributed to improved manufacturing overhead absorption and lower depreciation expenses.
  • Consolidated Operating Loss: $28.3 million, a decline from $32.7 million operating income in 2023, impacted by decreased revenues and reduced operating expenses.
  • Consolidated Net Loss: $19.5 million, compared to net income of $53.7 million in 2023, reflecting the significant drop in revenues.
  • Net Income Per Share: $0.26 loss per share on a fully diluted basis, compared to $0.72 income per share in 2023.

Business Highlights

  • Revenue Segments: The company's diagnostics products, including HIV, HCV, and Syphilis tests, saw a 3% increase in revenue, driven by higher international HIV revenues, particularly in Africa, despite lower sales in Asia.
  • Revenue Segments: Sample Management Solutions revenue decreased by 6%, impacted by reduced consumer demand for genomics collection devices and a decline in the microbiome market.
  • Revenue Segments: COVID-19 Diagnostics revenue experienced a significant 82% decline due to decreased sales of InteliSwab® tests following the fulfillment of U.S. government procurement contracts and lower overall demand for COVID-19 testing.
  • Geographical Performance: The company noted higher international HIV revenues primarily driven by customer ordering patterns in Africa, offset by lower sales into Asia.
  • New Product Launches: The company acquired Sherlock Biosciences, expanding its innovation pipeline with Sherlock’s molecular diagnostics platform, which is in Phase 3 clinical trials and expected to provide rapid results suitable for OTC usage.
  • New Production Initiatives: The company made progress on consolidating its manufacturing footprint by utilizing the Opus Way facility for a more significant portion of its manufacturing and distribution needs, including re-shoring capacity to the United States.
  • Future Outlook: The company expects further declines in COVID-19 testing revenues in 2025 due to the fulfillment of government contracts and reduced demand.
  • Future Outlook: The company plans to discontinue its risk assessment product line, with minimal sales expected through the first half of 2025 as it fulfills existing customer orders.

Strategic Initiatives

  • Strategic Initiatives: In 2024, the company undertook several strategic initiatives, including the wind-down and exit from its Diversigen molecular services business and the Novosanis operations in Belgium. It also led a Series B financing round and entered into strategic distribution agreements with Sapphiros, investing $30 million to expand its diagnostic test offerings. Additionally, the company acquired Sherlock Biosciences to enhance its molecular diagnostics platform.
  • Capital Management: The company repurchased shares to satisfy tax withholdings related to employee stock awards, with a total of 3,323 shares repurchased during the last quarter of 2024. The company has a share repurchase program authorized up to $25 million, with $11,984,720 remaining available. Cash and cash equivalents decreased from $290.4 million in 2023 to $267.8 million in 2024, primarily due to investments in Sapphiros and the acquisition of Sherlock. The company did not pay any dividends and does not anticipate doing so in the foreseeable future.
  • Future Outlook: The company expects research and development expenses to increase in 2025 as it invests in the development of Sherlock's test for CT/NG and other innovative projects. It plans to continue its strategic focus on expanding its diagnostic test offerings and enhancing its innovation pipeline through acquisitions and strategic partnerships.

Challenges and Risks

  • Market Risks: Changes in the genomics market, particularly the maturing ancestry segment, have led to volatile sales. The company is attempting to expand its molecular revenues both domestically and internationally, but success is uncertain due to market acceptance, funding availability, and regulatory constraints.
  • Operational Risks: The company anticipates a continued decline in revenues from its InteliSwab® COVID-19 Rapid Test due to reduced COVID-19 prevalence and the expiration of public health emergency declarations. Additionally, the company's inability to expand international sales and manage challenges in manufacturing processes could negatively impact revenue expectations.
  • Regulatory Risks: The company must maintain compliance with FDA Emergency Use Authorizations (EUAs) for its COVID-19 tests, which are subject to government discretion. Failure to comply with regulatory requirements could result in production suspensions or recalls, adversely affecting financial performance.
  • Emerging Risks: The company's acquisition of Sherlock Biosciences introduces integration risks, including potential loss of key employees and disruption of ongoing business. The success of this acquisition depends on achieving developmental and operational synergies.
  • Supply Chain Risks: The company relies on sole-source suppliers for critical components, which poses a risk if these suppliers are unable to meet obligations. Disruptions in the global supply chain could also impact the company's ability to deliver products.
  • Intellectual Property Risks: The company may face intellectual property disputes, which could increase costs and limit its ability to sell products or use certain technologies.
  • Management Strategies: Management is focused on addressing these challenges by expanding sales of genomics products, pursuing strategic acquisitions, and enhancing manufacturing capabilities. However, the success of these strategies is contingent on market conditions and regulatory approvals.
  • Market Risk Disclosures: The company is exposed to foreign currency exchange rate fluctuations, which may adversely affect its financial results. Additionally, changes in government funding and customer ordering patterns pose risks to revenue stability.

SEC Filing: ORASURE TECHNOLOGIES INC [ OSUR ] - 10-K - Mar. 07, 2025

data.title: Guild Holdings Co SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Guild Holdings Co, a growth-oriented mortgage company, has released its annual 10-K report, showcasing significant financial improvements and strategic initiatives aimed at expanding its market presence. The company, operating through its subsidiary Guild Mortgage Company, focuses on delivering personalized mortgage-borrowing experiences and has made substantial strides in both financial performance and business operations over the past year.

Financial Highlights

Total Revenue: $1,049.7 million, reflecting a 60.2% increase from the previous year, driven by higher loan origination fees and gain on sale of loans.

Net Income: $96.9 million, a significant improvement from a net loss of $39.1 million in the prior year, attributed to increased revenue and improved operating efficiency.

Net Income Per Share: $1.56, compared to a diluted loss per share of $0.64 in the previous year, indicating a strong recovery in profitability.

Adjusted EBITDA: $134.8 million, up from $74.8 million, highlighting improved operational performance and cost management.

Business Highlights

Business Model: Guild operates as a growth-oriented mortgage company with a relationship-based loan sourcing strategy, focusing on delivering personalized mortgage-borrowing experiences through its subsidiary, Guild Mortgage Company.

Geographical Expansion: Guild has expanded its retail origination operations to 49 states and the District of Columbia, with approximately 440 office locations, enhancing its national footprint.

Sales Units: For the years ended December 31, 2024 and 2023, Guild originated $23.0 billion and $14.4 billion, respectively, in retail mortgage loan originations, reflecting a significant increase in sales volume.

Product Offerings: Guild offers a diverse range of loan products, including those eligible for sale or securitization to secondary market participants such as Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac, and Ginnie Mae, among others.

Servicing Portfolio: As of December 31, 2024, Guild serviced approximately 370,000 loans with an aggregate unpaid principal balance of approximately $93.0 billion, indicating growth in its servicing operations.

Acquisitions: In 2024, Guild completed the acquisition of certain assets of Academy Mortgage Corporation, extending its market share across its national footprint and supporting its growth strategy.

Technology Platform: Guild's proprietary integrated platform for servicing and origination enhances loan officer productivity and fosters repeat business, providing a competitive edge in the mortgage market.

Future Outlook: Guild is well-positioned to meet or exceed projected industry growth in mortgage originations, with plans to continue expanding its business in new local communities and increasing its presence in existing geographic areas.

Strategic Initiatives

Strategic Acquisitions: Guild Holdings Company pursued strategic acquisitions of four mortgage lenders in 2024 and 2023 to expand its business into new local communities and increase its presence in existing geographic areas. These acquisitions were funded with cash on hand and borrowings totaling approximately $17.0 million in 2024 and $8.0 million in 2023.

Capital Management: Guild Holdings Company maintained a robust capital management strategy by utilizing warehouse lines of credit and notes payable to fund loan originations. As of December 31, 2024, the company had eleven loan funding facilities with a total facility size of approximately $2.7 billion and combined outstanding balances of approximately $1.4 billion. Additionally, the company had three notes payable facilities totaling $750.0 million, with $300.0 million outstanding. The company also repurchased 83,355 shares of its Class A common stock at an average price of $14.11 per share, with $10.0 million remaining available for repurchase under the authorized program.

Future Outlook: Guild Holdings Company plans to renew its warehouse lines of credit maturing in the next twelve months and continue its strategy of leveraging acquisitions, organic recruiting, and potential partnerships to meet or exceed market growth forecasts. The company aims to maintain its liquidity and capital resources to support ongoing operations and strategic initiatives, with a focus on sustainable growth and enhancing shareholder value.

Challenges and Risks

Market Risks: Changes in interest rates and U.S. monetary policies can adversely affect profitability. Rising interest rates have reduced origination volumes and increased competition, while decreasing rates could lead to a loss of servicing revenues.

Operational Risks: The company is highly dependent on warehouse lines of credit, and any termination or reduction could impact liquidity. Additionally, cybersecurity threats pose significant risks to data integrity and operational continuity.

Regulatory Risks: Compliance with complex legal and regulatory frameworks is critical. Changes in GSE guidelines or failure to comply with fair lending laws could have adverse effects. The potential for increased regulation of nonbank mortgage companies could also impact operations.

Emerging Risks: The geographic concentration of loan originations in the Northwest U.S. could expose the company to regional economic downturns. The impact of climate change and natural disasters on properties and insurance costs is also a growing concern.

Management Strategies: Management acknowledges the challenges posed by the current economic environment, including inflation and interest rate volatility. Strategies to address these include expanding the retail channel through acquisitions and enhancing technology infrastructure to improve operational efficiency and client service.

Market Risk Mitigation: Guild is exposed to interest rate risk, which affects the market value of loans and servicing rights. The company employs hedging strategies to mitigate this risk, but these are complex and may not fully offset adverse changes. The reliance on third-party service providers also introduces additional risks related to data privacy and security.

SEC Filing: Guild Holdings Co [ GHLD ] - 10-K - Mar. 07, 2025

data.title: El Pollo Loco Holdings, Inc. SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
El Pollo Loco Holdings, Inc., a leading player in the limited service restaurant (LSR) segment specializing in fire-grilled chicken and Mexican-inspired entrees, has released its 2024 10-K report. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges it faces in the current market environment.

Financial Highlights

  • Total Revenue: $473.0 million, reflecting a 0.9% increase from the prior year, driven by an increase in franchise revenue and franchise advertising fee revenue.
  • Company-operated restaurant revenue: $396.3 million, a slight decrease of 0.5% from the prior year, primarily due to the sale of company-operated restaurants to franchisees.
  • Operating Income: $41.2 million, representing an increase of 3.5% from the previous year, attributed to cost management and efficiency improvements.
  • Net Income: $25.7 million, a marginal increase of 0.5% from the prior year, supported by higher operating income.
  • Net Income Per Share: $0.86, reflecting an increase in profitability per share compared to the previous year.

Business Highlights

  • Revenue Segments: The company operates 498 domestic restaurants, with 173 company-operated and 325 franchised locations. Additionally, there are 10 licensed restaurants in the Philippines. The menu features a variety of Mexican-inspired entrees, with bone-in chicken meals and Mexican-inspired entrees accounting for 41% and 52% of company-operated restaurant sales in 2024, respectively.
  • Geographical Performance: The majority of the company's restaurants are located in California, with additional locations in Arizona, Nevada, Texas, Utah, Colorado, and Louisiana. The company also has a presence in the Philippines with 10 licensed restaurants.
  • Sales Units: In 2024, the company-operated restaurants generated average annual sales per restaurant of approximately $2.3 million. The company opened four new stores in 2024, two of which were franchised and two were company-owned.
  • New Product Launches: The company continues to innovate its menu with seasonal product offerings such as Double Chicken Tostadas and Fire-Grilled Burritos, and limited-time offers like Double Pollo Fit® Bowls.
  • Future Outlook: The company plans to drive growth through its strategic plan, focusing on brand differentiation, hospitality, digital engagement, unit economics, and national expansion. The company expects to continue investing in its loyalty and delivery programs and aims to expand its restaurant base, with future development led by franchisees.

Strategic Initiatives

  • Expansion Plans: El Pollo Loco plans to expand its business by opening one to two new company-operated restaurants in California and eight to nine new franchised restaurants in 2025. The company is focusing on increasing customer frequency, attracting new customers, and improving per-person spend to drive comparable restaurant sales growth.
  • Five-Pillar Growth Strategy: The company is executing a five-pillar growth strategy that includes enhancing brand recognition, adopting a hospitality mindset, prioritizing digital initiatives, optimizing unit economics, and pursuing national expansion.
  • Capital Management: The company has been actively managing its capital through a share repurchase program, under which it repurchased 535,628 shares for approximately $5.6 million in 2024. The company also completed a significant repurchase of 2.5 million shares from its largest stockholder, Freeman Spogli, for $26.6 million in August 2023. Furthermore, El Pollo Loco has been managing its debt through the 2022 Revolver, with $71.0 million outstanding as of December 25, 2024, and has reduced its revolver loan balance from $84.0 million in 2023. The company has also terminated most obligations under the income tax receivable agreement, reducing future cash outflows.
  • Future Outlook: In 2025, El Pollo Loco expects to incur between $30.0 million and $34.0 million in capital expenditures, focusing on new restaurant construction and investments in existing restaurants, including remodeling and technology enhancements. The company plans to fund these expenditures primarily through operating cash flows. Additionally, the company aims to continue its strategic initiatives to drive growth and improve operational efficiencies, while navigating macroeconomic challenges such as inflation and potential tariff impacts.

Challenges and Risks

  • Operational Risks: The company faces several operational risks, including the potential failure to open new restaurants or enter new markets successfully. This could be due to factors like site selection, financing, and regulatory approvals. The competitive landscape is intense, particularly in the greater Los Angeles area, which is a highly competitive market for Mexican-inspired quick-service restaurants (QSRs).
  • Economic Conditions: Economic conditions such as inflation, interest rates, and consumer spending patterns pose significant risks. The company is also vulnerable to changes in trade policies and tariffs, especially those affecting imports from Mexico, Canada, and China, which could increase costs for produce and supplies.
  • Labor Costs: Labor costs are a major concern, particularly with the implementation of AB 1228 in California, which raised the minimum wage for fast food workers to $20 an hour. This increase in labor costs could adversely affect profitability if not offset by other measures.
  • Public Health Crises: The company is also exposed to risks related to public health crises, such as the COVID-19 pandemic, which have previously disrupted operations and could do so again in the future.
  • Market Risks: The company is exposed to market risks such as fluctuations in commodity prices, particularly for chicken, which is a key ingredient. Environmental factors, including climate change, could exacerbate these risks by affecting supply chains and increasing costs.
  • Management Strategies: The company is implementing strategies to mitigate these risks, such as menu price adjustments, operational efficiencies, and strategic marketing initiatives to strengthen brand positioning and customer loyalty.

SEC Filing: El Pollo Loco Holdings, Inc. [ LOCO ] - 10-K - Mar. 07, 2025

data.title: Cryoport, Inc. SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Cryoport, Inc., a leading provider of temperature-controlled supply chain solutions for the life sciences industry, has released its 2024 10-K report. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges it faces in a highly competitive market. This summary highlights the key aspects of Cryoport's performance and strategic direction as detailed in the report.

Financial Highlights

Total Revenue: $228.4 million, a decrease of 2.1% compared to the previous year due to decreased demand for cryogenic systems.

Gross Margin: $99.6 million, representing 43.6% of total revenue, a slight increase from 42.6% in the previous year, driven by improved cost management.

Net Loss: $114.8 million, increased by 15.2% from the previous year, primarily due to impairment charges and increased SG&A expenses.

Net Loss Attributable to Common Stockholders: $122.8 million, reflecting a 14.1% increase from the previous year, impacted by the same factors affecting net loss.

Business Highlights

Revenue Segments: Life Sciences Services revenue increased by 6.6% to $153.7 million, driven by growth in BioStorage/BioServices and Commercial Cell & Gene therapy revenue, demonstrating strong demand for services offerings.

Revenue Segments: Life Sciences Products revenue decreased by 16.2% to $74.7 million, primarily due to decreased demand for cryogenic systems.

Geographical Performance: The Americas contributed 55.3% of total revenue, EMEA accounted for 26.1%, and APAC contributed 18.6%, reflecting a stable geographical distribution of revenue.

New Product Launches: The Cryoport ELITE™ -80°C Gene Therapy Shipper was launched in the second quarter of 2023, developed in collaboration with a leader in the gene therapy space.

New Product Launches: The Cryoport Express® Cryogenic HV3 Shipping System was introduced in the first quarter of 2025, offering optimized hold times and compliance with airline requirements.

New Production Launches: A new state-of-the-art IntegriCell™ facility was launched within the Houston campus in October 2024, addressing critical aspects of the supply chain for cell-based therapies.

Sales Units: Cryoport supported 701 clinical trials globally at the end of 2024, with 81 in phase 3, representing an increase from 675 trials at the end of 2023.

Future Outlook: The company plans to leverage Tec4Med technology across many of Cryoport’s products and services in the coming years, aiming to optimize operations and build a more resilient business.

Future Outlook: Cryoport intends to continue expanding its global presence and capabilities to support the growing demand for temperature-controlled supply chain solutions in the life sciences industry.

Strategic Initiatives

Strategic Initiatives: Cryoport is focusing on expanding its supply chain initiatives, geographic expansion, and technology investments to support anticipated growth. The company launched the IntegriCell™ platform, a standardized integrated apheresis cryopreservation and distribution solution for cell therapies, and opened a new state-of-the-art facility in Houston to optimize the supply chain for cell-based therapies.

Capital Management: Cryoport has been actively managing its capital through repurchase programs. In 2024, the company repurchased $185 million in aggregate principal amount of the 2026 Convertible Senior Notes, resulting in a net gain of $18.5 million. The company also has a repurchase program authorized through December 31, 2027, with $73.9 million of repurchase authorization available. As of December 31, 2024, Cryoport had cash and cash equivalents of $45.3 million, short-term investments of $216.5 million, and working capital of $277.0 million.

Future Outlook: Cryoport plans to continue incurring significant expenses for the foreseeable future as it invests in new supply chain initiatives, geographic expansion, and technology. The company expects to finance its operations through sales of equity securities and debt instruments and believes its current cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments will satisfy operational and capital requirements for at least the next twelve months. The company does not intend to pay cash dividends in the foreseeable future, preferring to reinvest in business development.

Challenges and Risks

Competitive Industry: The company operates in a highly competitive industry, particularly in the cell and gene therapy market, which requires stringent distribution standards supported by informatics. The company's success depends on its ability to innovate and maintain a competitive edge in technology-centric supply chain solutions.

Global Operations Risks: The company faces significant risks related to its global operations, including changes in foreign currency exchange rates, political and economic instability, and regulatory changes. The unpredictability of foreign currency exchange rates and potential changes in U.S. and international tax laws could materially affect the company's financial condition.

Procurement Risks: The company is also exposed to risks associated with the procurement of components from third-party manufacturers. Delays or increased costs in obtaining these components could adversely affect business operations and customer satisfaction.

Product Liability: Product liability is a significant risk, especially when products are used in human reproductive medicine. The company maintains product liability insurance, but claims exceeding coverage could materially impact financial conditions.

COVID-19 Impact: The COVID-19 pandemic has highlighted vulnerabilities in global supply chains and workforce participation, which could continue to affect the company's operations and financial performance.

Sales and Marketing: Management has identified the need to enhance sales, marketing, and distribution capabilities to support revenue growth. The company plans to expand its strategic alliances and improve its global marketing and sales network.

Inventory Management: The company is also focused on managing its equipment and inventory purchasing decisions based on customer demand forecasts. Inaccurate forecasts could lead to excess or obsolete inventory, affecting operating results.

Market Risks: The company is exposed to market risks, including fluctuations in foreign currency exchange rates, which could adversely impact financial results. The company relies on its Cryoportal® software platform for operations, and any failure in critical information systems could harm its reputation and future revenues.

Cybersecurity Risks: The company faces cybersecurity risks, with increasing frequency and sophistication of cyberattacks. Despite implementing security measures, the company acknowledges that no system is failsafe, and breaches could have significant financial and reputational impacts.

SEC Filing: Cryoport, Inc. [ CYRX ] - 10-K - Mar. 07, 2025

data.title: AM Best revises Arch’s issuer credit rating outlook to positive
data.description:
(The Insurer) - AM Best has revised the outlooks to positive from stable for Arch Capital and its subsidiaries’ long-term issuer credit ratings, and affirmed the Bermudian (re)insurer's A-plus financial strength rating with stable outlook.

The revision of the ICRs’ outlook to positive applies to the aa-minus rating of Arch Reinsurance and its strategic affiliates, and the a-minus rating of Bermuda-based ultimate holding company Arch Capital Group.

AM Best said that Arch’s ratings, along with its ICR outlooks revision to positive from stable, are based on the (re)insurer's historically strong operating performance.

“Though the reinsurance market has grown more favorable, particularly in the period after the January 1, 2023 renewal season, Arch has a proven track record, even in softer market years, of producing superior underwriting results and investment returns,” it said.

The rating agency added that Arch has continued to differentiate itself from its peers in the recent market with more favourable underwriting conditions.

The positive outlooks on the ICRs reflect AM Best's expectation that the group will continue to outperform its peers through profitable underwriting and “robust” investment returns, as it supports recent strategic acquisitions and continues to enhance its market position.

data.title: Correction to These Smart Cameras Spot Wildfires Article on March 2 — WSJ
data.description: AI-equipped cameras alerted a human-staffed operations center after the system detected a fire in Orange County on Dec. 4. "These Smart Cameras Spot Wildfires Before They Spread," at 9 a.m. ET on March 2, incorrectly said the system alerted a human-staffed command center at the University of California, San Diego.

data.title: US Equity Indexes Fall This Week as Fed's Influence Arrives Too Late to Recover From Tariffs-Led Sell-Off
data.description:
US equity indexes fell this week as the Federal Reserve's message on Friday that it can still wait for more clarity on the impact of fiscal policy decisions failed to outweigh a sell-off earlier this week when data signaled economic weakness and trade tariffs increased uncertainty.

* The S&P 500 closed at 5,770.20 on Friday, versus 5,954.50 a week earlier. The Nasdaq Composite stood at 18,196.22 compared with 18,847.28 a week prior. The Dow Jones Industrial Average ended at 42,801.72 end-of-play, versus 43,840.91 a week ago.

* Fed Chair Jerome Powell reminded investors that monetary policy is well-positioned to act as needed. "If the economy remains strong but inflation does not continue to move sustainably toward 2%, we can maintain policy restraint for longer," Powell said. "If the labor market were to weaken unexpectedly or inflation were to fall more quickly than anticipated, we can ease policy accordingly."

* Nonfarm payrolls rose less than forecast in February, and the unemployment rate rose. TD Economics said growth will likely soften over the coming months as federal layoffs continue to rise and ongoing trade policy uncertainty weighs on near-term hiring intentions. Layoff plans in the US increased to 172,017 jobs in February, the highest monthly total since July 2020, driven by government sector cuts, outplacement firm Challenger, Gray & Christmas said.

* The Trump Administration's indecision on tariffs levied on Canada and Mexico unsettled markets after President Donald Trump suspended 25% tariffs on goods covered by an existing free-trade agreement by the three companies until April 2, clouding the outlook for companies' supply costs.

* Morgan Stanley revised US economic growth lower for 2025 and 2026, saying facts have changed. "We now see higher inflation in 2025 with a more pronounced and sooner reacceleration in goods prices. The mix of firm inflation and low unemployment could put the Fed in a bind."
